stem: Find the radius of convergence \( R \) of the power series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n^{[[s]]}\left(\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)^{n} x^{n} \).
solution: The coefficient is \( c_n = n^{[[s]]}\left(\frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)^{n} \), and \( \lim_{n\to\infty}\sqrt[n]{|c_n|} = \frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \). Hence \( R = \dfrac{1}{[[a]]/[[b]]} = \dfrac{[[b]]}{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the limit \( L = [[a]]/[[b]] \) itself; the radius is its reciprocal \( 1/L \).
This ignores the coefficient \( [[a]] \) in the ratio.
This ignores the base \( [[b]] \) in the ratio.
This multiplies \( a \) and \( b \) instead of dividing.
This is off by one from the correct radius.
This is off by one from the correct radius.
This is off by two from the correct radius.

stem: Find the radius of convergence \( R \) of the power series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{n}\left(\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)^{n} x^{n} \) by applying the ratio test to the coefficients.
solution: With \( c_n = \dfrac{1}{n}\left(\frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)^{n} \), the ratio test gives \( \lim_{n\to\infty}\left|\dfrac{c_{n+1}}{c_n}\right| = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} = L \). The radius of convergence is \( R = 1/L = \dfrac{[[b]]}{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the ratio-test limit \( L \) itself; the radius is its reciprocal \( 1/L \).
This ignores the coefficient \( [[a]] \) in the ratio.
This ignores the base \( [[b]] \) in the ratio.
This multiplies \( a \) and \( b \) instead of dividing.
This is off by one from the correct radius.
This is off by one from the correct radius.

stem: Find the radius of convergence \( R \) of the power series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dbinom{2n}{n}\left(\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)^{n} x^{n} \).
solution: With \( c_n = \binom{2n}{n}\left(\frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)^{n} \), the ratio test gives \( \lim_{n\to\infty}\left|\dfrac{c_{n+1}}{c_n}\right| = 4\cdot\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \) (the central binomial coefficient contributes a factor of \( 4 \)). The radius is \( R = \dfrac{1}{4\cdot[[a]]/[[b]]} = \dfrac{[[b]]}{4[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the factor of \( 4 \) contributed by the central binomial coefficient.
This forgets the factor of \( 4 \) AND inverts \( a/b \).
This uses the ratio-test limit \( L=4a/b \) itself instead of its reciprocal.
This uses a factor of \( 2 \) instead of \( 4 \) for the binomial growth rate.
This is off by one from the correct radius.
This is off by one from the correct radius.

stem: The power series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{n}\left(\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)^{n} (x-[[c]])^{n} \) is centered at \( x=[[c]] \) with radius of convergence \( R = [[b]]/[[a]] \). Ignoring endpoint behavior, what is the right endpoint of its interval of convergence?
solution: The interval of convergence is centered at \( x=[[c]] \) and extends \( R=\dfrac{[[b]]}{[[a]]} \) in each direction, so the right endpoint is \( [[c]] + \dfrac{[[b]]}{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gives the LEFT endpoint (subtracts \( R \)) instead of the right endpoint.
This gives the radius itself, forgetting to shift by the center \( c \).
This inverts \( a/b \) before adding to the center.
This is just the center, ignoring the radius entirely.
This is off by one from the correct endpoint.
This is off by one from the correct endpoint.

stem: Find the radius of convergence \( R \) of the power series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left(\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)^{n} x^{2n} \) (note the exponent is \( 2n \), not \( n \)).
solution: Let \( y=x^{2} \); the series is \( \sum \left(\frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)^{n} y^{n} \), which converges for \( |y| < \dfrac{[[b]]}{[[a]]} \), i.e. \( x^{2} < \dfrac{[[b]]}{[[a]]} \). Hence \( R = \sqrt{\dfrac{[[b]]}{[[a]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to take the square root after substituting \( y=x^2 \).
This inverts \( a/b \) and forgets the square root.
This inverts the ratio inside the square root.
This squares the ratio instead of taking its square root.
This is off by one from the correct radius.
This is off by one from the correct radius.
